inline_review:
commit fe1fbd4406701631b78364d7528b29e5982bdd54
Author: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

Extend the contended_release tracepoint to queued spinlocks and queued
rwlocks. Use the arch-overridable queued_spin_release() to ensure the
tracepoint works correctly across all architectures.

> diff --git a/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h b/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h
> --- a/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h
> +++ b/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h
> @@ -129,12 +130,29 @@ static __always_inline void queued_spin_release(struct qspinlock *lock)
>  }
>  #endif
>  
> +DECLARE_TRACEPOINT(contended_release);
> +
> +extern void queued_spin_release_traced(struct qspinlock *lock);
> +
>  /**
>   * queued_spin_unlock - unlock a queued spinlock
>   * @lock : Pointer to queued spinlock structure
> + *
> + * Generic tracing wrapper around the arch-overridable
> + * queued_spin_release().
>   */
>  static __always_inline void queued_spin_unlock(struct qspinlock *lock)
>  {
> +	/*
> +	 * Trace and release are combined in queued_spin_release_traced() so
> +	 * the compiler does not need to preserve the lock pointer across the
> +	 * function call, avoiding callee-saved register save/restore on the
> +	 * hot path.
> +	 */
> +	if (tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)) {

Does embedding this tracepoint directly into the inline unlock fast path leak
the GPL-only contended_release symbol to non-GPL modules? 

Since queued_spin_unlock() is consumed by basic spinlock APIs, this might
cause standard spinlocks to fail linking for proprietary modules with an
unknown symbol error.

Could this also inject instrumentable code into noinstr sections and CPU idle
loops? 

Because queued_spin_unlock() acts as the underlying implementation for
arch_spin_unlock(), it is eventually consumed by raw_spin_unlock(). This is
deliberately used in noinstr sections to safely bypass lockdep and tracing.

Invoking tracepoint_enabled() here could result in objtool build failures.
Also, if invoked deep in the CPU idle loop where rcu_is_watching() is false,
this might trigger RCU lockdep splats or a use-after-free, since the standard
tracepoint isn't safe for idle.

> +		queued_spin_release_traced(lock);
> +		return;
> +	}
>  	queued_spin_release(lock);
>  }

[ ... ]

> diff --git a/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c b/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> --- a/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> +++ b/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> @@ -90,3 +90,19 @@ void __lockfunc queued_write_lock_slowpath(struct qrwlock *lock)
>  	trace_contention_end(lock, 0);
>  }
>  EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_write_lock_slowpath);
> +
> +void __lockfunc queued_read_unlock_traced(struct qrwlock *lock)
> +{
> +	if (queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock))
> +		trace_contended_release(lock);

Will this fire the tracepoint multiple times for a single contention event?

If multiple readers hold the read lock while a writer is queued on the
wait_lock, queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock) evaluates to true.

It appears every reader that subsequently drops the lock will trigger
trace_contended_release(lock), even though only the final reader that drops
the count to zero actually unblocks the writer.

> +	__queued_read_unlock(lock);
> +}
> +EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_read_unlock_traced);
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Adding `tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)` to the inline unlock fast paths breaks the module ABI for non-GPL modules by leaking a GPL-only symbol.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The `contended_release` tracepoint is exported strictly as a GPL-only symbol via `EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(contended_release)` in `kernel/locking/mutex.c`.
2. The patch embeds `tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)` directly into `queued_spin_unlock()`, `queued_read_unlock()`, and `queued_write_unlock()`, which are defined as `__always_inline` or `inline` in the generic headers.
3. These primitive functions back foundational locking APIs like `spin_unlock()` and `read_unlock()`. When these are inlined into a caller's translation unit, the static branch reference to the `__tracepoint_contended_release` symbol is directly compiled into the caller's code.
4. Consequently, any non-GPL or proprietary out-of-tree module that uses basic spinlocks will inherit a dependency on this GPL-only symbol. The kernel module loader will refuse to link it, failing with an 'Unknown symbol' error and effectively restricting standard spinlock APIs to GPL-only modules."},{"problem":"Inlining a tracepoint into the `queued_spin_unlock` fast path injects instrumentable code into `noinstr` sections and CPU idle loops, causing `objtool` build failures and RCU Use-After-Free vulnerabilities.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. `queued_spin_unlock()` serves as the underlying implementation for `arch_spin_unlock()` on major architectures (e.g., x86, arm64). This primitive is consumed by `raw_spin_unlock()`, which is deliberately used in `noinstr` sections (such as NMI entry/exit, early exception handling) and CPU idle loops specifically to safely bypass lockdep and tracing.
2. By inserting `tracepoint_enabled()` and a call to the out-of-line `queued_spin_release_traced()` function, the patch violates `noinstr` constraints. `objtool` will flag this as an invalid call that leaves the `.noinstr.text` section, breaking the build.
3. Furthermore, when `raw_spin_unlock()` is invoked deep in the CPU idle loop, `rcu_is_watching()` is false. Invoking the standard `trace_contended_release()` tracepoint in this context (instead of a safe `_rcuidle` variant) will bypass RCU protection for the tracepoint callback array, triggering 'RCU used illegally in idle' lockdep splats and exposing the system to a Use-After-Free if the tracepoint array is modified concurrently."},{"problem":"The `contended_release` tracepoint erroneously fires multiple times for a single `rwlock` contention event because every reader triggers the tracepoint upon unlocking while a writer waits.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. In `queued_read_unlock()`, the fast path evaluates `queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock)`, which returns true as long as a writer is queued on the `wait_lock`.
2. If multiple readers hold the read lock concurrently while a writer begins contending, every single reader that subsequently calls `read_unlock()` will evaluate the contention check as true and invoke `trace_contended_release(lock)`.
3. However, only the final reader that decrements the `lock->cnts` word to zero actually unblocks the writer and functionally releases the lock. 
4. Tracing every reader drop violates the 1:1 mapping of contention events to actual lock handoffs, creating severe logic errors in tracing statistics and flooding the tracing ring buffer with spurious release events."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit fe1fbd4406701631b78364d7528b29e5982bdd54

> +	__queued_read_unlock(lock);
> +}

"}
